Implementation Runbook: Microsoft Purview DLP & Data Governance Lab

Report by Presh Williams

Fictional business: Northwind Cinemas Ltd (a general UK business handling staff and customer data)

Regulatory framing. The lab is a general UK business handling staff and customer data. Controls map to UK data protection law and recognised security frameworks: UK GDPR / DPA 2018 (as amended by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025), Cyber Essentials, ISO/IEC 27001 (Annex A.8), ISO/IEC 42001, and EU GDPR. The EU AI Act is kept only as the AI anchor for the leakage analysis.

Status tags. [CORE] = configured and evidenced in this build · [DESIGN-ONLY] = designed and documented, with the licensing/onboarding dependency stated (not configured) · [STRETCH] = optional.

Synthetic data. Every document, identifier, and value used in this lab is fictional or non-issued. Nothing is real personal data.

1. Overview & thesis

This lab builds a working data classification and loss-prevention setup in Microsoft Purview for a fictional UK business, Northwind Cinemas Ltd, using synthetic data in an isolated test area. It covers a four-tier classification scheme, sensitivity labels, auto-labelling, and DLP policies across Exchange, SharePoint, and OneDrive, all run in audit or simulation mode. Endpoint DLP and AI/Copilot controls are documented as designs rather than configured, because of licensing.

The thesis is about where data governance is heading. Most DLP asks whether sensitive data is leaving the organisation. As businesses adopt AI, a second question matters just as much: is sensitive data being over-exposed inside the organisation, reaching a person or system that has access but no business need? A tool like Microsoft 365 Copilot can read whatever the user can open, so an over-shared payroll file, or a customer list a leaver still has access to, can end up summarised in an answer. The same data can also be pasted into a consumer AI tool outside the tenant, where governance ends.

The control for both directions is the same: classify the data once, then act on that classification wherever the data tries to move, including AI. That is the Purview labels-plus-DLP model this runbook implements and evidences. Section 7 sets out the leakage analysis and the framework mapping; the sections before it show the controls being built.

2. Environment & scope CORE

The lab runs inside an existing Microsoft 365 tenant. This is the "Option B" approach: reuse a live tenant, but isolate all lab work with synthetic data and tight scoping. Northwind Cinemas Ltd is fictional, and every document and identifier is fabricated or non-issued. The aim of this section is to show that the lab has zero production impact by design, not by luck.

The contained test area:

  • A dedicated private SharePoint team site, LAB-Purview-DLP-TEST, holds all test content, at https://[tenant].sharepoint.com/sites/LAB-Purview-DLP-TEST. The tenant domain is redacted in published screenshots.
  • A single fictional test user, John Doe, is the subject of every policy and a member of the test site.
  • Four synthetic documents form the test payload (see Section 3 for the tier mapping).

The isolation discipline:

  • Synthetic data only. Fabricated UK NI numbers, a non-issued NHS test number, and standard non-issued test payment card numbers. No real personal data is present.
  • Scoped policies. The label and auto-labelling policies are scoped to John Doe and the test site. DLP runs in simulation or audit mode, so nothing is blocked in production.
  • Sanitised evidence. Screenshots are redacted before publishing: tenant name and domain, admin display name, and real UPNs are removed. John Doe and the synthetic data stay visible.
  • Planned cleanup. Test policies, labels, the test site, and the trial licence are retired at the end of the lab.

Licensing. The tenant's base plan is Microsoft 365 Business Standard, which does not include Purview sensitivity labels or DLP. The gap was closed with a one-month Microsoft 365 E5 Information Protection and Governance trial, assigned to John Doe alongside the base plan. This is captured as a real finding in Section 4, because hitting it is a common early blocker.

3. Data classification scheme CORE

The scheme is the backbone of the lab. Every label, auto-labelling rule, and DLP policy implements it. Tiers run from least to most sensitive: Public, Internal, Confidential, Highly Confidential.

The four tiers:

  • Public. Approved for unrestricted release; disclosure causes no harm. Example: a marketing brochure.
  • Internal. Day-to-day business information for people inside the organisation; limited harm if exposed. Example: an internal memo.
  • Confidential. Sensitive business or personal information where disclosure would cause real harm, including most PII. Example: an HR record with NI numbers.
  • Highly Confidential. The most sensitive information, where disclosure would cause severe harm; strictly need-to-know. Example: a customer list with payment card numbers.

Handling matrix:

Control point Public Internal Confidential Highly Confidential
External sharing Allowed Discouraged (tip/audit) Blocked (exception only) Hard-blocked
External email Allowed Warn Block / justify Block
Endpoint USB / unmanaged upload Allowed Audited Blocked* Blocked*
AI / Copilot processing Allowed Sanctioned AI only Excluded by default* Fully excluded*
Encryption applied by label No Optional Yes* Yes*
Auto-label trigger None Default / manual PII SITs Payment / financial SITs

* Subject to licensing. Endpoint DLP, label-based encryption, and AI/Copilot controls need E5 / E5 Compliance / Copilot licensing. Where unavailable, the design is documented and the dependency noted.

Mapping the synthetic documents to tiers:

Document Target tier How the label is reached
Public brochure Public Manual / default
Internal memo Internal Manual
HR record (NI numbers) Confidential Auto-label via NINO detector
Customer list (card numbers) Highly Confidential Auto-label via Credit Card detector

4. Sensitivity labels & auto-labelling CORE

Four labels implement the scheme one to one. They were created, published to the test user, and verified working in Outlook and Word.

The labels:

Label Priority Content marking Encryption
Public 0 None No
Internal 1 None No
Confidential 2 Footer + watermark Designed, not applied*
Highly Confidential 3 Footer + watermark Designed, not applied*

* Label-based encryption is left off in this lab and documented as design-only (see below). The scope for all four labels is Files & Emails.

Publishing and mandatory labelling. The labels are published through a single policy scoped to John Doe. The policy also enforces mandatory labelling:

  • A label is required on documents and emails.
  • The default label is Public on both, so new content starts classified at the safe, lowest tier.
  • Lowering a label (a downgrade) requires the user to give a justification, which is logged.

This was verified live: a new Outlook message arrived pre-labelled Public with the full four-label picker available.

Auto-labelling. Two auto-labelling policies classify content by its sensitive info type. Both run in simulation mode, scoped to the test SharePoint site, using default confidence and instance-count thresholds:

  • LAB - Auto-label Confidential (NINO): detects the U.K. National Insurance Number SIT and proposes the Confidential label.
  • LAB - Auto-label Highly Confidential (Card): detects the Credit Card Number SIT and proposes the Highly Confidential label.

Both simulations completed, and the results matched the scheme exactly:

  • HR record (NI numbers) would be labelled Confidential.
  • Customer list (card numbers) would be labelled Highly Confidential.
  • The brochure and memo matched nothing and were left alone.

Encryption (design-only). Sensitivity labels can apply encryption and usage rights to the two sensitive tiers. This is left as design in the lab. The reasons are licensing and the risk of locking real content inside a live tenant. For a scoped lab, the safer choice is to mark and classify without enforcing encryption, and to document the encryption design rather than apply it.

5. DLP policies: rollout approach + per-workload

Rollout philosophy: audit first, then enforce. DLP is rolled out in stages. The policy in this lab runs in simulation / audit mode without notifications, scoped to the test area. It detects and records sensitive content, but it does not block anything or show users a policy tip. This is deliberate. A real rollout watches in audit mode first to size the impact and tune out false positives, then moves rules to enforcement. The handling matrix in Section 3 defines the enforcement end-state; this lab builds and evidences the detection foundation, and documents the enforcement design.

The policy. A single DLP policy, LAB - DLP UK PII (audit), covers Exchange, SharePoint, and OneDrive. Teams was left out to keep scope tight. It has two rules, each tied to a tier in the scheme:

  • NI number detected: the U.K. National Insurance Number SIT. Maps to the Confidential tier's handling rules.
  • Card number detected: the Credit Card Number SIT. Maps to the Highly Confidential tier's handling rules.

The configured action is detect and record, plus an alert to the administrator. There is no block action while in audit mode, by design.

Simulation results. The simulation scanned 145 items and found 6 matches, all on the test SharePoint site. OneDrive returned 0, and Exchange is evaluated in real time (covered in Section 8). The 6 matches are the two sensitive files seen across three scan passes each, not six separate files:

  • HR record matched the NI-number rule (3 NI numbers detected).
  • Customer list matched the Card-number rule.
  • Brochure and memo produced no matches, as intended.

Viewing the raw sensitive values requires the "Data Classification Content Viewer" role, which even an admin does not hold by default. That is a useful least-privilege detail: detection and value-inspection are separated.

5.1 Email (Exchange) [CORE]

Exchange DLP evaluates outbound email in real time. This was demonstrated live in the incident walkthrough (Section 8): an outbound email carrying the customer list triggered the Card-number rule, even though the user had labelled the email only Public.

Designed enforcement end-state, per tier:

  • Confidential: external send is blocked or requires a logged justification (policy tip, then block in enforce mode).
  • Highly Confidential: external send is blocked outright.

5.2 SharePoint / OneDrive [CORE]

SharePoint and OneDrive content is scanned at rest. The simulation results above are the evidence: the two sensitive files in the test library were detected and matched to the correct rules.

Designed enforcement end-state, per tier:

  • Confidential: external sharing blocked except through an approved, justified exception.
  • Highly Confidential: external sharing hard-blocked, with no self-service override.

5.3 Endpoint [DESIGN-ONLY]

Endpoint DLP controls what happens to labelled content on a managed device: copy to USB, upload to an unmanaged cloud location, or print. The design for the two sensitive tiers:

  • Confidential: copy to removable media and upload to unmanaged cloud are blocked; printing is audited.
  • Highly Confidential: copy to USB, unmanaged upload, and print are all blocked.

Dependency. Endpoint DLP needs Microsoft 365 E5 (or E5 Compliance) and devices onboarded to the compliance portal. Neither is in scope for this lab, so endpoint is documented as design only.

6. AI / Copilot data-leakage controls DESIGN-ONLY

This section designs the controls that stop sensitive labelled data being surfaced to AI, whether that is Microsoft 365 Copilot inside the tenant or a consumer AI tool outside it. They are written as designs, not configured: the relevant features (Purview DSPM for AI, and DLP for Microsoft 365 Copilot) need E5 and Microsoft 365 Copilot licensing, which is out of scope for this lab. The detection engine they rely on is the same one already proven against email and SharePoint in this build.

The concrete risk. The synthetic customer list is labelled Highly Confidential and holds test card numbers. If Copilot were enabled and that file sat in a library a user could open, a prompt such as "summarise our customer payment details" could surface that data to someone with technical access but no business need. The design below prevents that.

The controls follow the per-tier AI handling rules from the classification scheme (Section 3):

  • Public. No restriction. Available to AI as normal.
  • Internal. Allowed within sanctioned, tenant-bound AI such as enterprise Copilot. Blocked from unsanctioned or consumer AI tools.
  • Confidential. Excluded from AI processing by default. A DLP-for-Copilot rule withholds Confidential-labelled content from Copilot responses. The file can still be found and opened directly by an authorised user, but it is not fed into AI summaries.
  • Highly Confidential. Fully excluded from any AI processing. Never surfaced to Copilot, and blocked from being uploaded or pasted into any external AI tool.

How this would be configured (design):

  • A DLP policy using the Microsoft 365 Copilot and Copilot Chat location, with a sensitivity-label condition for the Confidential and Highly Confidential labels and the action set to restrict Copilot from processing that content. Excluded files can still be cited, but their content is not used in the response.
  • Purview DSPM for AI for visibility of which sensitive items are touched by AI prompts, providing the monitoring evidence.
  • Endpoint and browser DLP rules (the same family as Section 5.3) to block paste or upload of labelled content into consumer AI sites.

Dependency. All of the above need Microsoft 365 E5 (or E5 Compliance) plus Microsoft 365 Copilot licensing, and the endpoint and browser portion also needs onboarded devices. The design is complete and the mechanism is proven elsewhere in this lab; only the licensing gate prevents configuration.

7. AI-Era Data Leakage Analysis (the thesis section)

Section 1 set out the problem: internal over-exposure of sensitive data to AI, in both directions. This section maps the lab's controls to the rules that expect them.

What this lab proves, and what it only designs

The detection engine is proven; the AI-specific use of it is designed. The lab showed the engine working end to end. The U.K. National Insurance Number and Credit Card Number detectors found the synthetic HR record and customer list, auto-labelling proposed the right tiers, and DLP caught the customer list leaving in an outbound email that the user had labelled only "Public." Content inspection caught what the user's label missed. The AI/Copilot controls in Section 6 use the same labels and the same detectors on the AI path. They are design-only because they need extra licensing (E5 / Copilot), not because the mechanism is untested.

UK GDPR / DPA 2018 (as amended by the DUAA 2025)

The controls implement three security duties, which the 2025 amendments leave intact:

  • Article 5(1)(f) (integrity and confidentiality). Met by detecting PII such as NI and NHS numbers and restricting its movement by content, not by trusting the user's label.
  • Article 25 (data protection by design and by default). Met by mandatory labelling with a safe default of Public, and auto-labelling that classifies PII without relying on the user.
  • Article 32 (security of processing). Met by labels and DLP as the technical and organisational measures, including the designed label-based encryption and the audit-first rollout.

EU AI Act (used as an anchor, not a claimed obligation)

Northwind is a general UK business, not assumed to be a provider or deployer of high-risk AI, so the Act is a benchmark here rather than a duty:

  • Article 9 (risk management). Reflected in naming AI over-exposure as a documented risk with controls against it.
  • Article 10 (data and data governance). The closest fit: labels make classification explicit, and the designed AI controls govern which labelled data can reach AI at all.
  • Article 15 (accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity). Supported by keeping sensitive data out of AI processing, which lowers the leakage surface.

ISO/IEC 42001

The lab aligns with these readiness themes:

  • Knowing where AI is used and what data is shared with it (§1).
  • The data-side inventory an AI inventory is checked against (§2).
  • A documented escalation route from detection to response, via the incident-triage walkthrough (§3).
  • This analysis as the data-leakage risk assessment (§4).
  • Monitoring evidence from Activity Explorer and the DLP simulation, with the audit-first rollout as the improvement loop (§7).

8. DLP Incident-Triage Walkthrough CORE

This is a worked example of a single incident, run end to end against the lab's own detection. It shows the analyst response, not just the click path.

Scenario. John Doe downloads the Highly Confidential customer list from SharePoint and emails it, as an attachment, to an external recipient. He labels the email only Public. The external recipient is a domain under our own control, which keeps the test self-contained. In audit mode the email sends without being blocked, which is the expected behaviour for a detection-only policy.

The triage walkthrough:

  1. Detection. The Card number detected rule fired on an outbound Exchange email from John Doe, recorded in Activity Explorer at 14:34 on the test date, under policy LAB - DLP UK PII (audit). This is a real-time Exchange detection, separate from the at-rest SharePoint scan in Section 5.
  2. Triage and severity. The content is Highly Confidential (payment card data) and it is leaving the organisation to an external recipient. That combination is high severity: top classification tier, external egress.
  3. Investigation. Activity Explorer gives the who, what, where, and when: the file (customer list), the rule (Card number detected), the user (John Doe), the location (Exchange), and the timestamp. The email itself was labelled only Public, so the user under-classified it, and content inspection caught the card data regardless. The same view also recorded the mandatory default label (Public) being applied to the email. Note that the accompanying "Endpoint devices" label entry is label telemetry, not Endpoint DLP enforcement.
  4. Response and containment. In this lab the policy is in audit mode, so the response is detect, record, and alert the administrator. The email is not blocked. Under the enforcement end-state designed in Section 5, the same detection would block the send, show the user a policy tip, and require a justification.
  5. Reporting. The incident is recorded. If this were real personal data, this is the point to assess it against UK GDPR Articles 33 and 34: whether it is a personal-data breach reportable to the ICO within 72 hours, and whether affected individuals must be told. Here the data is synthetic, so nothing is reportable; the value is in demonstrating the assessment process.
  6. Closure and lessons. This is a true positive, handled proportionately. The lesson it illustrates is the thesis of the lab: user labelling alone is not enough, and content inspection is what catches under-classified data. Tuning options for a real rollout include adjusting confidence and instance-count thresholds to balance detection against false positives.

9. Framework Mapping Table CORE

Each control maps to specific clauses, not vague gestures. Where a framework has limited reach (Cyber Essentials over data classification, or ISO 42001 over non-AI controls), that is shown honestly rather than padded. The Status column separates what was configured in this build from what was designed.

Control Function Cyber Essentials ISO 27001:2022 (Annex A) ISO 42001 UK GDPR / DPA 2018 EU GDPR EU AI Act Status
Classification scheme + sensitivity labels Classifies data into four tiers Indirect A.5.12, A.5.13 §1 Art 5(1)(f), 25 Art 5(1)(f), 25 Art 10 (anchor) Configured
Mandatory labelling (default Public, downgrade justification) Forces a label by default Indirect A.5.13 §1 Art 25 Art 25 Art 10 (anchor) Configured
Auto-labelling (SIT to tier) Classifies PII and payment data automatically n/a A.5.12, A.5.13 §1 Art 5(1)(f), 25 Art 5(1)(f), 25 Art 10 (anchor) Configured
DLP detection (audit mode, Exchange/SP/OneDrive) Detects sensitive data movement n/a A.8.12 §7 Art 5(1)(f), 32 Art 5(1)(f), 32 Art 15 (anchor) Configured
DLP enforcement (block external share/email) Blocks external sharing of sensitive tiers Indirect A.8.12, A.8.3 n/a Art 5(1)(f), 32 Art 5(1)(f), 32 n/a Designed
Endpoint DLP (USB / upload / print) Controls labelled content on devices Indirect A.8.12 n/a Art 32 Art 32 n/a Designed
AI / Copilot exclusion controls Withholds labelled data from AI n/a A.8.12 §1, §4 Art 5(1)(f), 25, 32 Art 5(1)(f), 25, 32 Art 9, 10, 15 Designed
Audit logging & monitoring (unified audit log, Activity Explorer) Records label and DLP activity n/a A.8.15, A.8.16 §7 Art 5(2), 32 Art 5(2), 32 Art 12 (anchor) Configured
Incident-triage process Detection-to-response workflow n/a A.5.24-A.5.28 §3 Art 33, 34 Art 33, 34 n/a Demonstrated
Label-based encryption Encrypts the two sensitive tiers n/a A.8.24 n/a Art 32 Art 32 n/a Designed

Notes on the mapping:

  • Cyber Essentials covers baseline technical hygiene (firewalls, secure configuration, user access control, update and malware management). It does not directly address data classification or DLP, so entries are Indirect or n/a rather than forced.
  • ISO 27001 classification and labelling controls sit in Annex A.5 (A.5.12, A.5.13); DLP, logging, and cryptography sit in Annex A.8. The column header reads "Annex A" to cover both accurately.
  • ISO 42001 is AI-specific, so it maps to the AI-facing controls and the governance and monitoring themes, and is n/a for purely conventional DLP. The § references match the readiness themes used in Section 7.
  • EU GDPR mirrors UK GDPR on these articles; both are shown for completeness.
  • "Anchor" marks an EU AI Act reference used as a benchmark, consistent with Section 7's framing.

10. README pointer (for GitHub) CORE

The repository README is the 60-second version that sells the work and links into this runbook for the evidence. It should carry:

  • Problem statement. Sensitive data leaks not only outward, but inward, by being over-exposed to people and AI that have access without a business need.
  • The AI-era leakage thesis. Classify data once, then act on that classification everywhere it moves, including AI. (Section 1 and Section 7.)
  • What was built. A four-tier classification scheme, sensitivity labels with mandatory labelling, auto-labelling, and DLP in audit mode across Exchange, SharePoint, and OneDrive, all on synthetic data in an isolated test area.
  • What was designed, not configured. Endpoint DLP, AI/Copilot exclusion controls, and label-based encryption, each with its licensing dependency stated.
  • Frameworks mapped. UK GDPR / DPA 2018 (DUAA 2025), Cyber Essentials, ISO 27001:2022, ISO 42001, EU GDPR, and the EU AI Act as an anchor. (Section 9.)
  • What it demonstrates. Content inspection caught a Highly Confidential file leaving in an email the user had labelled only Public. That single result is the thesis in practice.
  • A link to this runbook for the full build and evidence.
